text: Thank you for the moderator. Good afternoon, everyone. I'm Dr. Li from Guangzhou, China. My topic is a novel technique for laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with CME, the caudal two cranial mites approach.
The complete CME procedure can include sub-dissection of visceral and parietal fascia, and ligation at the root of the central vessels, and radical lymph dissection.

There are three main approaches regarding laparoscopic red colectomy. That is the lateral-to-medial approach, and the medial-to-lateral approach, and the cranial-to-causal approach. Now we introduce a new one. That is the outer-to-cranial approach.

The surgeon confirms the presence of the tumor and moves the small intestine and omentum to the left upper quadrant. The appendix and last ileal loop are manipulated using syncytophoresis. The mesentery root is exposed and placed under tension, followed by sub-dissection at the caudal aspect of the mesentery root, accessing the vascular plane between the gastrocolic fissure and the visceral fissure of the mesocolon.

The surgeon continues the sub-dissection laterally, medially, and cephalically, separating the ascending colon from the retroperitoneal tissues, including the duodenum and pancreatic head. The dissection progresses towards the lateral attachment of the right colon.

The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) medially and identifies the pancreatic head cephalically. The duodenum and SMA are visible posteriorly. A gauze is placed in the dissected area for indication.
The surgeon performs blind dissection alongside sub-dissection techniques, progressing to the dissection of lymphatic tissue and division of vessels within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels and SMOE, incises the peritoneum along the ileocolic vessels, and extends the incision into the previously dissected right peritoneal space. Lymph nodes around the ileocolic vessels are dissected, and the root of the ileocolic vessels is identified, cut, and ligated.
The surgeon divides the ileocolic vessels, including both the vein and the artery, using surgical instruments to separate these structures from surrounding tissues.

Following the ligation and division of the ileocolic vessels, the surgeon continues the dissection from caudal to cranial direction. The right colic vessels are typically visible in their anatomical position, but in this patient, they are absent.

The surgeon carefully identifies the gastrocolic vessels, noting the absence of the superior right colic vessel. The middle colic vessels are exposed, and the right branches are cut. For an extended right hemicolectomy, the middle colic vessels are ligated and cut at their root.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the posterior wall of the stomach, with careful manipulation to separate it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic ligament along the greater curvature of the stomach. The transverse mesocolon is then dissected, and the hepatic flexure is mobilized. The dissection of the mesocolon continues in a downward direction.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the last lateral attachment of the right colon, fully freeing the entire right colon. The video shows the right colon after dissection, with various vessels visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis extracorporeally, completing the connection between the bowel segments.
